author: @ChristmasEve777
text: This idea works because the vapor is, indeed, pure water, as you said.  But you were wrong about what's contained in the area of the column higher than 34 feet when there is no sun/heat energy.  It is not a vacuum.  There will always be water vapor there whether the water is heated or not (unless it's frozen).  Water flash boils in a vacuum and will continue to to do so until either all the water has boiled away or enough pressure has been established.

So a series of outdoor shallow ponds would be required to heat the water and then the byproduct of desalination is brine which can not be returned to the sea. Ponds can be drained and brine removed with bulldozers.just need to identify a practical use for this brine.

replies.author: @bobnelly2716
replies.text: @ryanbeard1119 if my understanding is correct, it should be in a sealed system while operating and the same principals of heated distillation are in play. So once the water begins to boil, you will get a vapor. Then you condense the vapor in a collection container within the sealed system. The act of cooling the distillate from a vapor to a liquid creates vacuum, maintaining the vacuum with a lower physical water column. But I haven't tried this, so it is an assumption/hypothesis

author: @kenwebster5053
text:
I have toyed with this concept over the last decade or so.

So, when the water vapour condenses by releasing pressure, it should get hotter.  Therefore this heat can be exchanged to the inlet sea water to preheat it.  This would increase the efficiency of the plant, reducing external energy needs & the tendency of the desalinated supply to be hot and subject to evaporation on exposure to air.  How do we extract the near vacuum water vapour into a condenser to make this a continuous process?  It needs to be a continuous process to make the heat exchange efficient.

Could we simply have 2 towers, a fresh water side and a sea water side & pull vacuum simply by extracting water from the lower end of the fresh side?   We could make the towers concentric to facilitate heat exchange between them, providing sea temperature fresh water at the outlet. The towers could then simply be just be 2 concentric pipes open to each other at the top.  As we draw down the fresh water, condensation would occur at some point near the top of the jacket.

Then we have to think about salinity increasing in the inlet tube & exchange over saline water for sea water in some way.  Perhaps that could be gravity/density driven.  So, sea water would have come into the evaporation tube near the top so that over saline water could fall back to the sea via a 1 way valve. Maybe something like that?

The 2 water columns would nearly equalise pressure at the base of the towers, so we would not need a high pressure pump to drive this.

I suspect the fly in the ointment for practical operation would be dissolved gasses collecting in the vacuum chamber, causing loss of vacuum.  How would you remove them to maintain vacuum in an industrial scale continuous plant?

author: @CharlieMacklin1
text: Hello Robert, would you be interested in forming a team and joining the xprize competition for atmospheric carbon dioxide removal?  My idea is to use a similar technology as what you have demonstrated in this video, but with an above ground pipeline several miles long, on the coast of a desert area such as the Sahara.  The salt water pipeline could be designed in such a way that solar electric pumps the saltwater uphill in the pipeline full during the day, slowly heating it via the sun, then at night the pipeline is drained in such a way as to form a vacuum, allowing the collection of water vapors from branch pipes/collectors during the night.  The collected fresh water would then be drained to the soils where plants are continually grown, for the purposes of turning the desert into a habitable area while doing what plants do, removing CO2 from the atmosphere.

replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Very interesting. They are flash evaporating the water by spraying "warm" water from the surface into a vacuum chamber then condensing it by using the colder deep water. Basically the same process without the big column. I imagine the process could be easily sped up by preheating the water with a solar collector.

More info on this project:
http://www.newindianexpress.com/cities/chennai/Thermal-desalination-is-the-new-spring/2013/10/17/article1838983.ece

replies.author: @fjficm
replies.text: leerman22 But the atmospheric pressure is keeping it up there, therefore it balances out at equilibrium as stated at 34 feet of water (760 mm of mercury). No further energy required as the earth atmospheric pressure forces the water into the vacuum tube (Pressure = Force per unit area) and is countered by gravitational force where Force = mg, Pressure = density x height x g). To put it simply, water in an underseal vacuum at 34 feet is energetically at equilibrium. Therefore, making the tube higher than 34 feet gives it a space where water cannot reach thus creating a vacuum. Water at lower pressures boils at lower temperature (at the Himalayas it boils at around 60 Celsius). Therefore, they can take advantage of using a renewable energy like solar to reproduce potable water by steaming it at lower heat or energy levels required (like a factory reservoir) which will otherwise be wasted heat to the environment. Hope this is clear

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not in the least mate - it can be a wonky as you like but it does need a head of 34 feet - that is the top of the pipe and the bottom of the pipe need to be separated by a vertical distance of 34 feet but the length of pipe really doesn't matter - like a right angle triangle where the adjacent is the horizontal distance the opposite is the vertical distance and the hypotenuse is the length of pipe - the only critical thing is the length of the opposite which has to be 34 feet - the pipe length - the hypotenuse can be as long as you like

author: @joeglory425
text: It's not quite permanent. There are gasses dissolved in the water and the low pressure makes them come out of solution and collect in the vapor space. This would lead to a lowering of the water column over time. If you don't want to use a vacuum pump to purge the gases on a periodic basis what you could do instead is to use a pump to fill the entire column with water while at the same time venting the gases out the top with a vent valve and then when the column is completely full of water, shut the vent valve and then shut off the pump.
